lectureTitle: Block Ciphers and Modes of Operation

questions.stem: A block cipher takes a $k$-bit key $K$ and an $n$-bit plaintext $M$ and produces an $n$-bit ciphertext $C$. Mechanistically, why must $E_K$ behave as a *permutation* on $n$-bit strings when $K$ is fixed?

choices.text: So that the ciphertext can be larger than the plaintext to add randomness.
So that decryption is unambiguous: each ciphertext maps back to exactly one plaintext.
So that the cipher resists brute-force attacks on the key.
So that the same key can be used for encryption and authentication.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: A permutation is a bijection on $n$-bit strings. If $E_K$ were not bijective, two distinct plaintexts could map to the same ciphertext, and Bob would have no way to know which plaintext to recover. Bijectivity guarantees $D_K$ is well-defined.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Definition Mismatch** — The block cipher output is exactly $n$ bits, the same size as the input; randomness is added by modes of operation, not the cipher itself.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Goal Conflation** — Brute-force resistance comes from the size of the key space, not bijectivity.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Category Error** — Block ciphers provide confidentiality, not authentication; integrity needs MACs.

questions.stem: Slide 15 states that AES has a fixed 128-bit block size regardless of which key length is used. If a student says "AES-256 uses a 256-bit block," what is the precise correction?

choices.text: AES-256 uses a 192-bit block to balance the larger key.
AES-256 has a 256-bit block; the number always refers to the block size.
The 256 refers only to the key size; the block size is always 128 bits in AES.
AES has no fixed block size; it adapts based on the message.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: AES-128, AES-192, and AES-256 all share the same 128-bit block; only the key length and number of rounds change. The number in the name refers to key bits, not block bits.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Plausible Misconception** — There is no 192-bit AES variant for blocks; only key sizes vary.
distractorAnalysis.B: **Definition Swap** — Confuses key length with block length.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Absolutist Error** — Block ciphers are by definition fixed-block algorithms; modes of operation handle long messages.

questions.stem: A secure block cipher should be "computationally indistinguishable from a random permutation." In the distinguishing game, Eve gets two boxes: one is $E_K$ with a random key and the other is a uniformly random permutation. What is the security requirement?

choices.text: Eve must be able to identify $E_K$ in fewer than $2^n$ queries.
Eve cannot tell the boxes apart with probability significantly greater than $1/2$.
Eve must be unable to invert either box without the key.
The two boxes must produce identical output on all $2^n$ inputs.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Indistinguishability means Eve's advantage over a 50/50 coin flip is negligible. If she can detect any structure that distinguishes $E_K$ from a true random permutation, the cipher is broken.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Misframed Goal** — The game is about distinguishing, not key recovery.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Different Property** — One-wayness is a hash property; block ciphers are about indistinguishability.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Logical Impossibility** — Two distinct permutations cannot be identical on all inputs while being independently chosen.

questions.stem: Slide 13 walks through brute-forcing a 128-bit key using hardware that tries $10^{18}$ keys per second. Mechanistically, why is this calculation enough to declare the brute-force attack "infeasible" without further proof?

choices.text: Because $10^{21}$ seconds exceeds the age of the universe by many orders of magnitude, making the attack physically impossible within any meaningful timeframe.
Because the attack would consume too much electricity to be cost-effective today.
Because Moore's Law guarantees the hardware will improve before the attack finishes.
Because all keys must be tried in sequence; no parallelism is possible.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: $10^{21}$ seconds is roughly $3 \times 10^{13}$ years — about 30 trillion years, vastly longer than the ~$1.4 \times 10^{10}$-year age of the universe. The argument is about absolute time, not cost.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Weaker Argument** — Cost matters but isn't the formal reason; the time bound is the airtight one.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Reverse Logic** — Faster hardware would *help* the attacker, not hurt them.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False Premise** — Brute-force attacks are embarrassingly parallel; the calculation already assumes massive parallelism.

questions.stem: Slide 17 shows that a raw block cipher is not IND-CPA secure. Walk through Eve's winning strategy: she sends $M_0, M_1$, receives $C^* = E_K(M_b)$, then asks for the encryption of which message?

choices.text: A new random message and check whether the ciphertext is shorter than $C^*$.
$M_0$ again, then check whether the new ciphertext equals $C^*$. If yes, $b=0$; otherwise $b=1$.
$M_0 \oplus M_1$ to recover $K$ from the result.
$M_0 || M_1$ and look for repeated blocks in the output.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Block ciphers are deterministic: $E_K(M_0)$ always produces the same ciphertext. Eve queries $E_K(M_0)$ and compares to $C^*$. If they match, $b = 0$ with probability 1; otherwise $b = 1$.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Irrelevant** — All ciphertexts are exactly $n$ bits; length carries no information.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Non-mechanism** — XORing plaintexts and asking for that encryption does not reveal $K$ for a generic block cipher.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Wrong Test** — The IND-CPA game is decided by the single $C^*$ versus what Eve can derive about $b$, not by structural patterns inside concatenations.

questions.stem: In the ECB encryption diagram (Slide 25), each plaintext block $M_i$ is independently encrypted with the same key $K$ to produce $C_i$. What is the immediate visual consequence on the encrypted-penguin image (Slide 27)?

choices.text: The penguin outline disappears entirely because each block looks random.
The penguin outline remains visible because identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks.
The penguin appears upside-down due to bit-reversal.
The penguin is replaced with the AES round constants.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: ECB is deterministic per block. Large solid-colored regions of the image consist of many identical blocks, all of which encrypt to the same ciphertext block. The repeated pattern survives encryption and reveals the silhouette.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Opposite Outcome** — That is what CBC/CTR achieve, not ECB.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Nonsense Trap** — Block ciphers do not perform geometric inversions.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Irrelevant** — Round constants are AES internals, not visible in ciphertext output.

questions.stem: CBC encryption is defined $C_i = E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ with $C_0 = IV$. What specific role does the IV play in defeating the deterministic-output problem ECB suffers from?

choices.text: It is a checksum that detects tampering with the ciphertext.
It randomizes the input to the first block cipher invocation, so the same plaintext encrypted twice produces different ciphertexts.
It selects which AES key schedule to use for that message.
It pads the final block to a multiple of the block size.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: A fresh random IV makes $M_1 \oplus IV$ different on every encryption, so $C_1 = E_K(M_1 \oplus IV)$ differs every time. That randomness then chains forward through all subsequent blocks via $C_i = E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Goal Conflation** — IVs do not detect tampering; that requires a MAC.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Mechanism Error** — AES key schedule depends only on the key, never on the IV.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Different Concept** — Padding handles size, not randomness.

questions.stem: Slide 34 derives CBC decryption: $M_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$. Why does decryption use the *previous ciphertext block* rather than the previous plaintext block?

choices.text: Because decryption must operate on whatever the receiver actually has, and the receiver only has ciphertext until decryption is complete.
Because plaintext blocks are XORed with the key, so they cannot appear in the formula.
Because CBC alternates direction every block.
Because $D_K$ requires the previous round constants to be regenerated.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: The receiver parses ciphertext as $(IV, C_1, \dots, C_j)$ and has no plaintext yet. Algebraically, $M_i = D_K(E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})) \oplus C_{i-1} \oplus C_{i-1} = M_i$ — the previous ciphertext is the value Bob actually has.

distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Operation** — Block cipher encryption is not an XOR with the key.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Made-up Behavior** — There is no directional alternation in CBC.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Irrelevant Internal** — Round constants are deterministic given the key; nothing needs the previous plaintext.

questions.stem: PKCS-style padding pads with the *number* of padding bytes (e.g., 3 bytes of `0x03`). If a message is already an exact multiple of the block size, what does the padding rule require?

choices.text: No padding is added; the message is sent as-is.
A single zero byte is appended.
An entire dummy padding block is added so the unpadder can always strip exactly the indicated number of bytes.
The first block is duplicated to act as padding.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: If we appended nothing when the size already aligned, the unpadder would have no way to know whether the last byte is real or padding. Always appending a full padding block (e.g., 16 bytes of `0x10` for AES) makes unpadding unambiguous.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Ambiguity Trap** — Skipping padding makes the last bytes ambiguous to the unpadder.
distractorAnalysis.B: **Insufficient** — A single zero would conflict with messages that legitimately end in zero.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Nonsense Trap** — Duplicating the first block has no relationship to padding length.

questions.stem: Slide 39 shows Alice encrypting two three-block messages $P_1 P_2 P_3$ and $P_1 P_2 P_4$ with the *same* IV. What does Eve learn from observing the two ciphertexts?

choices.text: She recovers the secret key $K$.
She learns that the two messages share an identical prefix up to (but not including) the first differing block.
She learns the entire plaintext of both messages.
She learns nothing because CBC is IND-CPA secure even with IV reuse.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: With identical $IV$, $P_1 \oplus IV$ is identical for both messages, so $C_1$ is identical. Then $P_2 \oplus C_1$ is identical, so $C_2$ is identical. Once the plaintexts diverge, the chained ciphertexts diverge, so Eve sees the prefix-matching pattern but not the underlying values.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Catastrophic Overstatement** — IV reuse in CBC leaks prefix patterns, not the key.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Overstatement** — CTR with nonce reuse leaks plaintexts; CBC is not as catastrophic.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — IV reuse explicitly breaks IND-CPA because Eve can detect identical prefixes.

questions.stem: CBC encryption cannot be parallelized, but CBC decryption can. Mechanistically, why does the asymmetry exist?

choices.text: Encryption uses $E_K$, which is intrinsically sequential, while $D_K$ is parallel by construction.
Encrypting block $i$ requires $C_{i-1}$, which is itself an output, but decrypting block $i$ requires $C_{i-1}$, which the receiver already has as input.
The IV must be re-randomized between every encryption block.
Decryption skips the chaining step entirely.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Encryption depends on the *output* of the previous block (a value not yet computed), forcing sequencing. Decryption depends on the *input* ciphertext blocks, which are all received up front and can be processed in parallel.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Wrong Property** — $E_K$ and $D_K$ are equally parallel-friendly as primitives; the chaining is what matters.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Mechanism** — One IV per message, not per block.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — Decryption still XORs with $C_{i-1}$; chaining is preserved, just not on a forward dependency.

questions.stem: In the CTR diagram (Slide 50), the inputs to the block cipher are a (Nonce, Counter) pair, and the output is XORed with the plaintext. Which block cipher operation is used for *decryption* in CTR mode?

choices.text: $D_K$, the block cipher decryption function.
$E_K$, the block cipher encryption function.
Both $E_K$ and $D_K$, alternating per block.
Neither; CTR uses only XOR.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: CTR generates a keystream by encrypting nonce-counter pairs with $E_K$, then XORs that keystream with plaintext or ciphertext. Because XOR is its own inverse, decryption regenerates the same keystream with $E_K$ and XORs again — never invoking $D_K$.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Common Misconception** — Many students assume decryption uses $D_K$, but CTR converts a block cipher into a stream cipher, so only $E_K$ is needed.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Made-up Behavior** — There is no alternation in CTR.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Underspecified** — XOR alone is not enough; you still need $E_K$ to produce the keystream.

questions.stem: CTR mode does not require message padding, but CBC does. What property of CTR makes padding unnecessary?

choices.text: CTR pads internally with random bytes.
CTR produces a keystream the same length as the plaintext, and any extra keystream bits are simply discarded.
CTR uses variable-length block sizes that adapt to the message.
CTR requires the message to be exactly $2^{64}$ bytes.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Because CTR is essentially a one-time pad keyed by $E_K(\text{nonce}||\text{counter})$, you only need as many keystream bits as you have plaintext bits. Extra keystream is truncated, so the ciphertext length matches the plaintext length exactly.

distractorAnalysis.A: **False** — There is no internal padding in CTR.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — The block cipher block size is fixed at $n$ bits; CTR works around it.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Nonsense Constraint** — There is no such fixed message-length requirement.

questions.stem: Slide 56 warns that nonce reuse in AES-CTR is "catastrophic." Why is the failure mode for CTR worse than for CBC under analogous IV reuse?

choices.text: Because CTR uses a longer key, so failures cascade further.
Because CTR effectively becomes a two-time pad — XORing two ciphertexts cancels the keystream and exposes $M_1 \oplus M_2$.
Because CTR requires $D_K$ to recover plaintext, and $D_K$ leaks the key on reuse.
Because CBC has no IV, so there is nothing to reuse.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: If $C_1 = M_1 \oplus K_s$ and $C_2 = M_2 \oplus K_s$ for the same keystream $K_s$, then $C_1 \oplus C_2 = M_1 \oplus M_2$ — exactly the OTP key-reuse failure. Crib-dragging often recovers both messages.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Irrelevant** — Key length is unrelated to nonce reuse.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False Mechanism** — CTR uses $E_K$ only; $D_K$ is never invoked.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — CBC explicitly uses an IV; that's the entire point of Slide 39.

questions.stem: Slide 60 argues that even though CBC and CTR are theoretically equally secure, CBC is preferable when you are "paranoid about security." What human-factors argument supports preferring CBC?

choices.text: CBC is faster, so attackers have less time to act.
Under IV/nonce reuse — a likely real-world implementation mistake — CBC leaks only limited prefix information, while CTR fails completely.
CBC includes a built-in MAC for integrity.
CTR is patented and CBC is open-source.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Real-world IV/nonce mistakes happen. Defense-in-depth says: pick the mode whose worst-case-misuse failure is the least catastrophic. CBC's prefix leak is bounded; CTR's two-time-pad failure is total.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Opposite Fact** — CTR is faster (parallelizable on encryption); speed is not the security argument.
distractorAnalysis.C: **False** — Neither CBC nor CTR provides integrity; both need a separate MAC.
distractorAnalysis.D: **False** — Neither is patented; both are open standards.

questions.stem: Slide 58 draws a subtle distinction between IVs and nonces. What is the formal difference?

choices.text: IVs must be encrypted; nonces are sent in the clear.
IVs must be random and one-use; nonces only need to be unique (number used once), not necessarily random.
IVs are 128 bits; nonces are 64 bits.
IVs are used for authentication; nonces for confidentiality.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: An IV is randomly generated for each encryption (used in CBC). A nonce only needs to be unique across encryptions — it could be a counter, for example. The course uses the terms interchangeably, but the formal distinction matters for some constructions.

distractorAnalysis.A: **False** — Both are public; both are sent alongside the ciphertext.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Made-up Sizes** — Lengths depend on the algorithm, not the term used.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Goal Conflation** — Both are about confidentiality (preventing key/keystream reuse).

questions.stem: Slides 62–65 demonstrate Mallory changing "Pay Mal \$100" to "Pay Mal \$900" by flipping a single ciphertext byte from `0x58` to `0x50`. What is the precise mechanism?

choices.text: Mallory recovered the key by analyzing repeated bytes in the ciphertext.
Mallory XORed the target byte position with $0x01 \oplus 0x09 = 0x08$, which propagates through the keystream as $C' \oplus E_K(i) = 0x39$, the ASCII for '9'.
Mallory replaced the entire ciphertext block with a random one.
Mallory needed to know the key to perform this attack.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Because $C = M \oplus E_K(i)$, flipping bits in $C$ flips the same bits in the decrypted $M'$. To turn '1' (`0x31`) into '9' (`0x39`), flip the bit difference (`0x08`) — accomplished by XORing the ciphertext byte with `0x08`, changing `0x58` to `0x50`. No key knowledge required.

distractorAnalysis.A: **False** — Mallory never recovers the key; she exploits XOR linearity.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Wrong Granularity** — The attack is byte-precise; replacing whole blocks would scramble the message.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Critical Misunderstanding** — The whole point of the example is that Mallory does *not* need the key.

questions.stem: Slide 66 notes that altering a CBC ciphertext bit causes "some blocks to become random gibberish." Which property does this *NOT* provide?

choices.text: Confidentiality of the original plaintext.
Integrity, because Bob cannot prove that Alice did not intend to send gibberish.
IND-CPA security under proper IV usage.
Recoverability of the modified ciphertext block via inverse XOR.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Producing gibberish is not the same as detecting tampering. Bob has no way to distinguish "Alice sent gibberish" from "Mallory tampered with a real message," so CBC alone does not provide integrity.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Provided** — CBC with a proper IV does provide IND-CPA confidentiality.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Provided** — CBC is IND-CPA secure when the IV is fresh and random.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Mechanism, not goal** — XOR inversion is a property of the operation, not a goal CBC must satisfy.

questions.stem: You are designing a high-throughput streaming-video encryption system that processes data faster than it arrives. Padding latency must be zero, and decryption of arbitrary 1-second segments must be possible. Which mode best fits?

choices.text: ECB, because it has no chaining.
CBC, because it provides better security under reuse.
CTR, because encryption and decryption are parallelizable, no padding is required, and arbitrary segments can be decrypted independently.
Raw AES with no mode.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: CTR's no-padding requirement, full parallelism, and per-block independence make it ideal for streaming and random-access decryption. Random-access decryption is impossible in CBC because each block depends on the previous ciphertext.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Insecure** — ECB is not IND-CPA secure (Slide 27 penguin proves this).
distractorAnalysis.B: **Wrong Trade-off** — CBC needs padding and serial encryption; bad fit for streaming.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Not a real option** — Raw AES can only encrypt single 128-bit blocks.

questions.stem: After studying all four points (ECB insecurity, CBC chaining, CTR keystream model, lack of integrity in both), which architectural conclusion follows?

choices.text: Block ciphers in any mode are sufficient for both confidentiality and integrity if the key is long enough.
You should always pair an IND-CPA mode (CBC or CTR) with a separate MAC, or use an authenticated encryption mode like GCM, to obtain both confidentiality and integrity.
ECB is acceptable when the plaintext has no repeating blocks.
Stream ciphers and block ciphers are interchangeable in all settings.

questions.mechanisticExplanation: Lecture 03 establishes that confidentiality modes alone never provide integrity, motivating the next lecture's MAC and AEAD discussion. The systems-design takeaway is: never deploy CBC/CTR without a MAC.

distractorAnalysis.A: **Absolutist Trap** — Key length addresses brute force, not integrity.
distractorAnalysis.C: **Risky Heuristic** — Whether the plaintext has repeating blocks is hard to verify and changes over time; ECB is simply not safe to use.
distractorAnalysis.D: **Overgeneralization** — Stream and block ciphers have different operating modes, padding behavior, and integrity properties.
